title: Azure Logic Apps - Security, Access, And Managed Identity Conventions
applies_to: Using managed identity with Logic Apps for service-to-service access

confirmed_facts.fact: Logic Apps security guidance covers access control, data protection, encryption, secure communication, and network controls for workflows and connections.
Azure Logic Apps supports both system-assigned and user-assigned managed identities for authenticating access to Azure resources and services that support Microsoft Entra authentication.
A managed identity removes the need to store credentials, secrets, or Microsoft Entra tokens in workflow definitions or connection settings when the target service supports Entra authentication.
Managed identity authentication works only with connectors and destination services that support Microsoft Entra authentication.

In Standard logic app workflows, the default internal API connection authentication uses the system-assigned managed identity, and this connection authentication can be changed to a user-assigned identity for finer control.

For Standard logic app workflows in single-tenant Azure Logic Apps, network restrictions for connections follow Azure App Service-style restrictions and require connector-specific service tags and FQDN allowances.

implementation_notes: Prefer managed identity for Azure-native and Entra-integrated targets whenever the connector or destination supports it; this is the default secure automation pattern for Logic Apps.
Treat connection authentication separately for built-in and managed connectors. Some connectors can use managed identity directly while others still need connection resources or other auth methods.

For API-ingestion workflows that later persist to SharePoint or another Microsoft 365 target, verify whether the SharePoint connector path supports the desired auth model. If not, a Graph or HTTP path may be more predictable.

When documenting or generating Logic Apps solutions, always specify whether the workflow uses system-assigned or user-assigned managed identity and which Azure RBAC or resource permissions must be granted to that identity.
caveats: Managed identity does not automatically solve authentication for every connector; support is per-connector and per-target.
Single-tenant networking and access restrictions require more explicit connector egress planning than multitenant Consumption workflows.
Security design must account for both workflow runtime access and connector callback or webhook behavior.
